name: Chinese White
bio: Chinese painter of the Qing dynasty known as Chinese White created refined, carefully balanced ink landscapes and bird-and-flower compositions in the orthodox tradition of the Four Wangs. Working within the literati painting tradition, he developed careful, harmoniously balanced compositions based on the disciplined study of Song and Yuan dynasty masters. His work represents the conservative continuation of the Orthodox School's approach to Chinese landscape painting during the prosperous Qing period.

name: Yasuo Kuniyoshi
bio: Japanese-American modernist Yasuo Kuniyoshi brilliantly blended Japanese aesthetic sensibilities with American Regionalist themes, becoming a leading figure of the New York art scene between the wars. Trained at the Art Students League under Kenneth Hayes Miller and in Paris at the Academie de la Grande Chaumiere, he developed a uniquely personal style combining American subject matter with Japanese decorative flatness. He was the first living artist commissioned to paint a mural for the Museum of Modern Art.

name: T.C. Steele
bio: American impressionist T.C. Steele was the leading member of the Hoosier Group, creating luminous, sun-drenched landscapes of the Indiana countryside that earned him recognition as the foremost painter of the American Midwest. Trained at the Royal Academy in Munich and later in Paris, he returned to Indiana and established a studio and gardens in Brown County. His brilliant, colorful landscapes of the American Midwest brought European impressionist technique to bear on distinctly American subject matter.

name: Sin Yun-bok
bio: Korean genre painter Sin Yun-bok of the late Joseon period created vivid, intimate depictions of everyday life and romantic encounters under the well-known pen name Hyewon. A court painter who served as a member of the Royal Painting Bureau (Dohwaseo), his genre albums focused on the private, often erotic lives of the aristocracy and the world of kisaeng entertainers. His warm, occasionally scandalous depictions provide a uniquely intimate look into the social dynamics of 18th-century Korea. His candid depictions of aristocratic romance and kisaeng entertainers remain among the most culturally revealing works of Korean genre painting.

name: Cristóbal Rojas
bio: Venezuelan painter Cristobal Rojas studied in Paris alongside his compatriot Arturo Michelena, developing a somber, socially conscious realist style that made him one of his country's most important 19th-century artists. Trained at the Academie Julian in Paris under Jean-Paul Laurens, he combined French academic technique with a dark palette and deeply humanitarian themes influenced by his own suffering from tuberculosis. His premature death at thirty-three cut short a career of rising international acclaim.

name: Silva Porto
bio: Portuguese naturalist painter Silva Porto is regarded as the leading figure of the Silva Porto generation, celebrated for his plein-air landscapes of the Portuguese countryside that brought naturalism to Portugal. Trained at the Academy of Fine Arts in Lisbon and later in Paris and Rome, he introduced the Barbizon school's approach of painting directly from nature. His masterworks such as 'O Outono' (Autumn) exemplify the crucial transition from Romanticism to Naturalism in Portuguese art. His introduction of Barbizon-style plein-air naturalism to Portugal fundamentally transformed Portuguese landscape painting.

name: Jēkabs Kazaks
bio: Latvian modernist painter Jekabs Kazaks was a founding member of the Expressionist group Expressionisti, brilliantly fusing cubist and futurist influences with distinctively Latvian national themes. Trained at the Penza School of Art and the Kazan Art School, he enthusiastically embraced avant-garde European currents while maintaining a distinctively Latvian emotional character. Despite his tragic death from tuberculosis at only twenty-five, he is considered a pioneer of 20th-century Latvian art and a key figure in Baltic modernism.

name: Augusto Giacometti
bio: Swiss painter and stained-glass artist Augusto Giacometti, uncle of the great Alberto Giacometti, created vibrantly colored ornamental and semi-abstract symbolic works in the Art Nouveau and symbolist traditions. Trained in Zurich and later in Paris at the Academie Julian, he developed a brilliantly colored, increasingly abstract style that anticipated postwar lyrical abstraction and tachism. His magnificent stained-glass windows in churches and public buildings across Switzerland constitute one of the most significant bodies of 20th-century religious glass art.

name: Adolf Wölfli
bio: Swiss artist Adolf Wolfli created an immense, extraordinarily intricate body of drawings, collages, and musical compositions while institutionalized for mental illness at the Waldau clinic near Bern. Hospitalized from 1895 until his death, he developed a densely patterned visual universe incorporating autobiographical narrative, fantastical architecture, and elaborate musical notation. His vast body of work, championed by Jean Dubuffet as a foundational example of Art Brut, helped establish Outsider Art as a serious artistic category.

name: Sophie Taeuber-Arp
bio: Swiss artist, dancer, and designer Sophie Taeuber-Arp was a central figure of the Zurich Dada movement and a pioneer of geometric abstraction across multiple media. Trained in Zurich at the School of Applied Arts, she participated in Dada performances at the Cabaret Voltaire and co-founded the progressive constructivist art magazine Abstraction-Creation. Her clean, rhythmically composed geometric abstractions in painting, textile design, and interior design influenced the development of concrete art and non-objective painting.

name: Gheorghe Tattarescu
bio: Romanian painter and muralist Gheorghe Tattarescu studied in Rome and became a leading exponent of neoclassicism in his homeland, painting religious frescoes in Romanian churches and allegorical compositions. Trained at the Bucharest Academy and the Academy of Saint Luke in Rome, he introduced the Italian neoclassical style to Romanian religious and civic painting with refined draftsmanship. His dignified portraits of the Romanian revolutionary generation of 1848 document the founders of the modern Romanian state.

name: Josip Račić
bio: Croatian painter Josip Racic was a key member of the Munich Circle, a group of Croatian modernists who studied in Germany and brought advanced impressionist and post-impressionist techniques back to Croatian art. Trained at the Zagreb School of Crafts and the Munich Academy, he developed a refined, subtle, and intimate style of portraits and still lifes that showed the influence of Edouard Manet. His introspective, quietly powerful works and his suicide at twenty-three made him a legendary, tragic figure in Croatian modernism.

name: Fabian de la Rosa
bio: Filipino painter Fabian de la Rosa was the leading figure of the academic tradition in Philippine art, celebrated for his warm genre scenes, landscapes, and portraits that captured early 20th-century Filipino life with dignity. Trained at the Academy of Fine Arts in Manila and later in Paris, he became the first director of the University of the Philippines School of Fine Arts, training the next generation. His gentle, realist depictions of rural and urban life shaped the visual identity of the emerging Philippine nation.

name: Ahmed Sabri
bio: Egyptian painter Ahmed Sabri studied in Paris and became a pioneering figure of modern Egyptian art, blending academic realism with Orientalist themes and Egyptian national subjects. Trained at the Cairo School of Fine Arts and later at the Academie Julian in Paris, he returned to Egypt to become a professor and eventually director of the Cairo Museum of Modern Art. His elegant portraits of Egyptian notables and his role in founding modern Egyptian art education established him as a founding father of Egyptian painting.

name: Abul Hassan Khan Ghaffari
bio: Persian court painter Abu'l-Hasan Khan Ghaffari, honored with the imperial title Sani al-Molk by the Qajar court, created the monumental portrait cycle of the Naseri era and sumptuous illustrations for a Persian translation of 'One Thousand and One Nights'. Trained in Italy on a royal scholarship, he introduced European oil painting and perspective techniques to Iran while maintaining the decorative richness and jewel-like colors of the Qajar miniature tradition. His founding of the first modern art school in Iran laid the foundations for contemporary Iranian painting.
